18: edothe moi pasa exousia en ourano kai epi ges — to me has been given all authority in heaven and on earth — edothe (has been given, divine passive) grounds the authority in the Father's gift, not in Christ's inherent right alone (though the divine sonship implies it); the crucified one now speaks as the enthroned one. The scope is total (pasa exousia = all authority) and universal (en ourano kai epi ges = in heaven and on earth) — the Danielic authority of the Son of Man (Dan 7:14) now historically enacted in the post-resurrection commission.
20: idou ego meth hymon eimi pasas tas hemeras heos tes synteleias tou aionos — behold I am with you all the days until the completion of the age — idou (behold, a call to attention) introduces the climactic promise. Ego eimi (I am) is the divine-presence formula; pasas tas hemeras (all the days = every single day, not merely always in general) stresses the daily-accompanying reality. The frame of the Gospel (Immanuel = God with us, 1:23; I am with you always, 28:20) is the Matthean inclusio: the Gospel's subject is the presence of God incarnate, permanently dwelling with his people to the end of the age.
1: opse de sabbatōn tē̄ epiphōskousē̄ eis mian sabbatōn — after the Sabbath at the dawning toward the first day of the week — Opse sabbatōn (after the Sabbath — opse = late in, after; genitive of time) — Saturday evening after sunset, the beginning of Sunday in Jewish reckoning. Tē̄ epiphōskousē̄ (at the dawning, as it began to light) — present participle of epiphōskō (to dawn upon, to begin to shine). Eis mian sabbatōn (toward the first day of the week) = Sunday.
2: kai idou seismos egeneto megas — and behold a great earthquake occurred — Seismos megas (great earthquake) — the second earthquake of the Passion narrative (cf. 27:51). Angelos gar kyriou katabas ex ouranou kai proselthōn apekylisenτον lithon (for an angel of the Lord descending from heaven came and rolled away the stone) — apokyliō (to roll away from) — the stone is removed not to let Jesus out but to let witnesses in.
3: ēn de hē eidea autou hōs astrapē kai to endyma autou leukon hōs chiōn — his appearance was like lightning and his clothing white as snow — Eidea (appearance, form — related to eidon = to see) — the visible aspect. Astrapē (lightning) and chiōn (snow) are both extreme-brightness comparisons. The angel’s appearance echoes the Transfiguration’s luminosity (17:2).
4: apo de tou phobou autou eseisthēsan hoi tēroūntes kai egenēthēsan hōs nekroi — from fear of him the guards shook and became as dead men — Eseisthēsan (shook, the earthquake verb applied to persons) — the guards experience the same shaking as the earth. Hōs nekroi (as dead men) — the irony: the guards appointed to prevent resurrection become death-like while the dead one lives.
5: apokritheis de ho angelos eipen tais gynaixin — the angel answering said to the women — Apokritheis (answering — the Semitic convention for beginning speech; the women have not spoken, but the angel responds to their evident state). Mē phobeisthe hymeis (do not be afraid, you) — the hymeis (you) is emphatic contrast: the guards have collapsed in fear, but the women need not fear.
6: ouk estin hōde ēgerthē gar kathōs eipen — he is not here; for he was raised as he said — Ouk estin hōde (he is not here) — the negative declaration of absence precedes the positive declaration of resurrection. Ēgerthē (aorist passive: was raised) — divine passive; God raised him. Kathōs eipen (as he said) — the resurrection vindicates and fulfills the predictions of 16:21; 17:23; 20:19; 26:32. Deute idete ton topon (come see the place) — invitation to verify the empty tomb.
7: kai tachy poreuthesai eipate tois mathētais autou hoti ēgerthē apo tōn nekrōn — and go quickly and tell his disciples that he was raised from the dead — Tachy (quickly, at once) — urgency. Ēgerthē apo tōn nekrōn (was raised from the dead) — the full resurrection formula. Kai idou proagei hymas eis tēn Galilaian (and behold he goes before you to Galilee) — fulfilling 26:32; ekei auton opsesthe (there you will see him) — the Galilean appearance is promised.
8: kai apelthousai tachy apo tou mnēmeiou meta phobou kai charas megalēs — leaving quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy — The paradoxical combination phobou kai charas (fear and joy) is simultaneously true: the awesome reality of the angel and the empty tomb produces fear; the message of resurrection produces joy. Edramon apangelai tois mathētais autou (they ran to announce to his disciples).
9: kai idou Iēsous hypēntēsen autais legōn chairete — and behold Jesus met them saying: rejoice/greetings — Hypantaō (to meet, encounter) — the risen Jesus meets the women before they reach the disciples. Chairete (rejoice, hail, greetings — the standard Greek greeting, but here charged with new meaning: the resurrection joy). Hai de proselthousai ekratēsan autou tous podas kai prosekynēsan autō̄ (approaching they grasped his feet and worshiped him).
10: tote legei autais ho Iēsous mē phobeisthe — then Jesus says to them: do not be afraid — The same formula as the angel (v.5); Jesus repeats the assurance. Hypagete apangelate tois adelphois mou (go announce to my brothers) — adelphois (brothers) — Jesus calls the disciples brothers for the first time in Matthew, a post-resurrection relational redefinition. Hina apelthōsin eis tēn Galilaian kakei me opsontai.
11: poreuomenōn de autōn idou tines tēs koustōdias elthontes eis tēn polin apēngeilan tois archiereusin — while they were going some of the guard coming into the city announced to the chief priests — Tines tēs koustōdias (some of the guard) — not all; some go to the chief priests with their report. The guards’ report to Jewish leaders rather than Roman authorities suggests they were Jewish temple police rather than Roman soldiers, or that they understood the political implications.
12: kai synagentes meta tōn presbyterōn symboulion te labontes argyria hikana edōkan tois stratiōtais — assembling with the elders and taking counsel they gave a sufficient sum of silver to the soldiers — Hikana (sufficient, adequate — a commercial term) argyria (silver coins) — a bribe. Symboulion labontes (taking counsel) — the same deliberative term as 27:1; the leaders respond to the resurrection report with another conspiracy.
13: legontes eipate hoti hoi mathētai autou nyktos elthontes eklepsan auton — saying: say that his disciples coming by night stole him — The counter-narrative requires the soldiers to admit sleeping on duty (a capital offense in Roman military law), making the story implausible on its face — sleeping guards could not know who stole the body. The leaders must promise to protect the soldiers (v.14) precisely because the story is self-incriminating.
14: kai ean akousthē̄ touto epi tou hēgemonos hēmeis peisomen kai hymas amerim nous poiēsomen — and if this is heard before the governor we will persuade him and keep you free from anxiety — Peisomen (we will persuade) — the leaders accept responsibility for managing Pilate if the story reaches him. Amerim nous (free from anxiety, without care — from a + merimna = worry) — a legal promise of indemnity.
15: hoi de labontes ta argyria epoiēsan hōs edidachthēsan — taking the silver they did as they were taught — Edidachthēsan (were taught/instructed — aorist passive of didaskō) — the soldiers are given a narrative and instructed to propagate it. Kai diephēmisthē ho logos houtos para Ioudaiois mechri tēs sēmeron (and this story was spread among Jews until today) — diaphēmizō (to spread a report throughout) — a contemporary apologetic note.
16: hoi de hendeka mathētai eporeuthēsan eis tēn Galilaian eis to oros hou etaxato autois ho Iēsous — the eleven disciples went to Galilee to the mountain where Jesus had directed them — Hendeka (eleven) — the number after Judas’s death. Eis to oros (to the mountain) — significant mountain encounters throughout Matthew (5:1; 17:1; 24:3); the final commission on a mountain echoes the Sinai covenant. Hou etaxato autois (where he had appointed/directed them — tassō = to arrange, appoint, a military/administrative term).
17: kai idontes auton prosekynēsan hoi de edistasan — seeing him they worshiped but some doubted — Prosekynēsan (worshiped, full prostration) — the climactic act. Hoi de edistasan (but some doubted) — distazō (to hesitate, to be of two minds — from dis = twice; to be pulled in two directions). The honest acknowledgment of doubt among the worshipers is remarkable; the commission is given despite/to the doubters.
19: poreuthentes oun mathēteusate panta ta ethnē — going therefore disciple all the nations — Mathēteusate (aorist imperative of mathēteuō = to make disciples) is the main verb; poreuthentes (going), baptizōntes (baptizing), and didaskontes (teaching) are participles supporting the main command. Panta ta ethnē (all the nations) — universal scope completing 24:14. Baptizōntes autous eis to onoma tou patros kai tou huiou kai tou hagiou pneumatos — the Trinitarian formula, unique in Matthew; onoma (name, singular) with three persons.
